text: The laparoscopic view shows the trocar puncturing the large mucinous cystadenoma on the right side, causing the cyst to rupture. The surgeon aspirates the spilled contents, which appear slimy and sticky due to the mucin content.

The video shows the opening created by the trocar insertion over the cyst. The surgeon begins the cystectomy by aspirating the mucinous fluid from the abdominal cavity, retracting the cyst at various ends to facilitate fluid removal from the paracolic gutters.

The surgeon identifies the multi-loculated nature of the mucinous cystadenoma, noting that even after aspirating contents from one locule, several other smaller locules remain visible within the cyst.

The surgeon utilizes three ports for the laparoscopic cystectomy: two lateral ports and a central port above the suprapubic region. The ovarian cortex is cut open along the antimesenteric border, visibly stretching it into two parts to facilitate the cystectomy and reduce blood loss.

The surgeon uses a shearer to separate the thick ovarian cortex while gently performing the cystectomy. The ovarian cortex appears notably thick, likely due to the prolonged presence of the cyst collection.
The surgeon identifies and gently coagulates small bleeding vessels encountered during the cystectomy of a large mucinous cystadenoma, ensuring a clean surgical field.

The surgeon performs meticulous cystectomy on a large cyst using gentle traction and counter traction. The three-port technique is employed, with two ports accommodating needle holders that provide strong counter traction during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the remaining loculi and sub-loculi within the mucinous cyst, which are visibly projecting outward. The procedure aims to remove these loculi intact.

The surgeon carefully navigates dense adhesions between the cyst and the ovarian cortex at the hilar region, applying gentle traction and counter traction to avoid shearing venous connections and excessive bleeding.

The surgeon successfully enucleates the entire mucinous cyst intact, preserving the volume of the ovarian cortex. Minimal blood loss is observed, achieved by adhering to the dissection plane and coagulating small blood vessels to maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon completes the cystectomy and prepares to remove the specimen from the peritoneal cavity using an endobag. The endobag is positioned to safely contain and extract the specimen through the surgical ports.

The surgeon rolls open the endobag and introduces it into the pelvic cavity for stability. The specimen is placed into the open mouth of the endobag. Needle holders inserted through the two lateral ports provide stability and traction to the ovarian tissue, while scissors from the central port ribbonize the specimen. The specimen is extracted through the ten mm port in a single long stripe without damage or excessive pressure.

The surgeon thoroughly washes the peritoneal cavity with five percent dextrose normal saline, infiltrating approximately three to five liters to act as a mucolytic agent. Any residual mucoid collection is aspirated out. The ovarian volume is observed returning to normal size and is placed in the pouch of Douglas. The cystectomy bed is visible without any bleeding.
